sound_type: warning huff -- explosive exhalation through nose and mouth. 100-600 Hz broadband. De-escalation. 'I see you. Back off.' E=5
ecosystem: Forest (coniferous, mixed, deciduous), forest edge, riparian corridors. Year-round resident. Huff in any season when threatened.
season: Year-round. Most common spring (mothers with cubs) and fall (pre-denning foraging).
family: Ursidae (FIRST ENTRY -- bear family enters the engine)

coordinates.description: Black Bears throughout western Washington. In forests surrounding every major city. 25,000-30,000 statewide.

taxonomy_note.description: FIRST URSIDAE. The bear family enters the engine at degree 200. After Cervidae (195-199). New mammalian family.
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Ursidae: Black Bear Huff (200, E=5). First entry.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Ursus from Latin 'bear'. americanus for the Americas.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Ursus -- true bears. Black Bear, Brown/Grizzly Bear, Polar Bear.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Carnivora. First Carnivora entry. Despite the order name, Black Bears are primarily herbivorous.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Ursidae: first entry. New family at milestone degree 200.
taxonomy_note.class_note: Class Mammalia. 60-200 kg. Apex omnivore. Solitary.

song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Huff: 100-600 Hz. Jaw pop: 200-800 Hz. Growl: 60-300 Hz.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Huff: 0.3-1.0 sec. Repeated 2-5 times with 1-3 sec intervals.
song_metrics.note_structure: Single explosive exhalation per huff. Repeated pattern during sustained warning.
song_metrics.note_types: Huff (warning), jaw pop (threat), growl (aggression), woof (alarm), moan (contentment), cub cry (distress).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Repeated huffs at 1-3 second intervals. Often accompanied by head lowering and swaying.
song_metrics.timbre: Broadband, breath-like, percussive. Not tonal. Low-frequency emphasis. Honest signal of body size.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=5. Explosive and startling at close range (5-20 m). Short-range warning. Not a broadcast.
song_metrics.tempo: Huffs at 1-3 second intervals. May escalate to faster repetition if threat persists.
song_metrics.context: Warning/de-escalation. Bear has detected threat. Not aggressive -- attempting to avoid conflict.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Minimal. Huff acoustically similar across Black Bear populations. Size variation affects depth.
song_metrics.key_property: HUFF. Explosive warning exhalation. De-escalation. FIRST URSIDAE. E=5. ENERGY MATCH with Death Cab. v1.49.341.

pelage.overall: Black to dark brown (most common in PNW). Occasionally cinnamon or blonde. Shorter muzzle than Grizzly. No shoulder hump. Straight face profile.
pelage.diagnostic_mark: Black/dark brown fur. Straight face profile. No shoulder hump. Relatively short claws. Tan muzzle patch common.
pelage.seasonal_variation: Thicker coat in winter. May appear shaggier in spring (molting). Cubs: black, sometimes with white chest patch.

physical_description.overall: Adult: 60-200 kg (130-440 lbs). Males larger than females. 70-100 cm at shoulder. Strong, compact body.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: Black/dark brown. No shoulder hump (distinguishes from Grizzly). Straight face profile. Short curved claws.
physical_description.field_id: Medium-large black/brown bear without shoulder hump. In forest, forest edge, or (increasingly) suburban settings. If huffing: do not run. Speak calmly, back away slowly. The bear wants you to leave.

nodes.title: FIRST URSIDAE -- Bear Family Enters
nodes.description: New mammalian family at degree 200 milestone. Apex omnivore. Solitary. Intelligent. 25,000-30,000 in Washington. The huff as entry point.

nodes.title: Huff -- Explosive Warning Exhalation
nodes.description: 100-600 Hz broadband. 0.3-1.0 sec. At 5-20 m range. Often misinterpreted as aggression. Actually de-escalation.

nodes.title: De-escalation Vocalization
nodes.description: The huff is the opposite of aggression. An aggressive bear charges or jaw-pops. The huffing bear wants you to leave. Warning, not threat.

nodes.title: Urban-Wildland Interface
nodes.description: Bears in forests surrounding every PNW city. Suburban encounters increasing. Understanding bear communication essential for coexistence.

cross_references.relationship: CERVIDAE TO URSIDAE! Deer family (E=3-4) to bear family (E=5). New ecological niche.
SCALE COMPARISON! Elk bugle/roar (E=7) vs. bear huff (E=5). Display/combat vs. de-escalation.
BOTH E=5! Cow call (connection) and bear huff (avoidance). Same energy, opposite intentions.
ENERGY MATCH! Both E=5. Measured warning. Communicating something serious through control.
